narration: A set is like a memory board that only cares whether something has shown up.
callout: Even if cat appears twice in the list, the set would still only keep one cat.
decision: Use a set when presence is all you need.

narration: A map stores extra information, like a count or an index, for each key.
callout: The label is the key, and the stored information is the value.
decision: Use a map when you need more than yes-or-no memory.

predictionPrompt.prompt: If the job is “How many times did each thing appear?”, which tool fits best?

choices.label: Use a map
choices.feedback: Correct. Counting needs a key with a stored value.

choices.label: Use a set
choices.feedback: Not this time. A set forgets the count.

narration: Many slow nested-loop problems become faster once you remember the right thing in a map or set.
callout: Those four questions lead to the four chapter strategies: seen-before, counting, lookup by need, and matching between collections.
decision: The right memory tool helps you stop rescanning the whole list.

reflectionPrompt.prompt: Why can extra memory sometimes make a program faster?
reflectionPrompt.placeholder: Because the map or set can remember...
